questions.q: In a layered architecture, which layer typically handles business logic and orchestrates interactions between the presentation and data layers?
options: Presentation layer
Application layer
Domain/Business layer
Infrastructure layer
questions.explanation: The domain or business layer contains core business logic and serves as the intermediary between presentation and data access. The application layer handles use cases but delegates business rules to the domain layer. Presentation layer handles UI only.

questions.q: What is a primary advantage of hexagonal architecture compared to layered architecture?
options: It eliminates all external dependencies from the core domain
It allows the application core to be tested independently of UI and database implementations
It guarantees faster performance due to fewer abstraction layers
It automatically scales horizontally without configuration
questions.explanation: Hexagonal (ports and adapters) architecture isolates the core business logic from external concerns like UI, databases, and APIs, enabling unit tests without mocking those systems. Layered architecture couples these concerns more tightly. The other options misrepresent the benefits.

questions.q: In MVC architecture, when a user submits a form, what is the correct sequence of components involved?
options: View → Model → Controller → View
Controller → View → Model → Database
View → Controller → Model → View
Model → Controller → View → Model
questions.explanation: User interaction reaches the Controller, which modifies the Model, and then the View re-renders to display the updated Model state. Option 0 skips the controller initially. Option 1 has the wrong order. Option 3 begins with Model.

questions.q: Which statement best describes the trade-off between microservices and monolithic architectures?
options: Microservices eliminate all deployment complexity while monoliths are always tightly coupled
Monoliths are simpler to develop and deploy initially but harder to scale and modify; microservices distribute complexity across deployment and integration
Microservices are always cheaper to operate due to smaller individual services
Monoliths provide better data consistency because they share a single database
questions.explanation: Monoliths reduce initial complexity but scaling and change isolation become difficult. Microservices solve those problems but introduce distributed system challenges (deployment, testing, network latency). Options 0, 2, and 3 oversimplify these trade-offs.

questions.q: In CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation), commands and queries are separated. What is the primary benefit of this separation?
options: It eliminates the need for a database entirely
It allows the read model to be optimized independently of the write model, improving query performance
It guarantees that all data modifications complete within 100 milliseconds
It removes the need for authentication and authorization checks
questions.explanation: Separating commands (writes) from queries (reads) lets you optimize each path independently—reads can use denormalized views or caching, while writes focus on consistency. Options 0, 2, and 3 are incorrect or unrelated benefits.

questions.q: A legacy system has a single relational database shared by all modules. You want to move toward microservices. Which pattern helps preserve temporary stability while transitioning?
options: Strangler Fig pattern
Repository pattern
Service Locator pattern
Observer pattern
questions.explanation: The Strangler Fig pattern allows you to gradually replace parts of a legacy system by routing requests to new microservices while the old system still exists, enabling incremental migration. The Repository pattern is for data access abstraction. Service Locator and Observer address different concerns.

questions.q: What architectural problem does the API Gateway pattern solve in a microservices system?
options: It stores data more efficiently than relational databases
It provides a single entry point, reducing client complexity and handling cross-cutting concerns like authentication and rate limiting
It ensures all microservices run in the same process
It eliminates the need for message queues
questions.explanation: An API Gateway acts as a facade, providing one endpoint for clients to call, and centralizes authentication, rate limiting, logging, and request routing. Options 0, 2, and 3 are unrelated to the API Gateway's role.

questions.q: In a three-tier architecture, the middle tier becomes a bottleneck under high load. Which scaling approach is most appropriate without redesigning the entire system?
options: Add more application server instances behind a load balancer
Replace the relational database with a NoSQL database
Move all business logic to the client
Merge the presentation and application tiers into one tier
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling of the stateless application tier via load balancing distributes load effectively and is the standard solution. NoSQL doesn't address the tier bottleneck. Moving logic to the client or merging tiers introduces other problems.

questions.q: A team chose a monolithic architecture. Six months later, a small team needs to independently deploy a new feature without affecting other modules. What is the fundamental limitation they face?
options: Monoliths cannot use version control systems
All code shares a single deployment unit, so changes by any team risk the entire system
Monoliths do not support concurrent user requests
Object-oriented programming is not possible in monoliths
questions.explanation: The core constraint of monoliths is that all modules are compiled and deployed as one unit; a single bug in any module affects the whole system. The other statements are incorrect.

questions.q: Which architectural pattern is most suitable for a system where different business domains have distinct data consistency requirements, scalability, and team ownership?
options: Single database with layered application architecture
Microservices with per-service databases and API contracts
MVC pattern with a shared session store
Service Locator with a central registry
questions.explanation: Microservices allow each domain to choose its own database, consistency model, and deployment cadence, supporting independent team ownership and scaling. Layered architecture with a shared database creates tight coupling and consistency conflicts. MVC and Service Locator don't address domain separation.
